title: Consistency-guided Prompt Learning for Vision-Language Models

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new adaptation method for CLIP like large scale vision-language models for generalization benchmarks. Specifically, the authors propose 3 techniques to improve generalization of CLIP. Firstly, they observe that the main cause of poor generalization is the lack of consistency constraints between the learned embeddings and the original pretrained embeddings. To overcome this issue, consistency losses are used at the text side as well as the image side separately. Secondly, the inputs to the original models are perturbed with the help of augmentations and LLM captions for image and text side respectively. Lastly, the proposed method combines the adapter and prompt learning modules with-in the same architecture for improved performance. 

Extensive benchmark comparisons are conduced on 3 different generalization tasks where the proposed approach shows improvements against prior methods. Furthermore, ablation studies are provided for analyzing contributions of each component separately and motivating the design choices.
reviews.strengths:
1) This paper addresses an important aspect of generalization of pre-trained CLIP like models for downstream task adaptation. Most of the prior methods struggles to achieve good performance on unseen classes and datasets, while this method explicitly add training constraints to mitigate the issue.
2) The proposed framework is motivated fairly, and the strength of its individual components have been demonstrated clearly in the ablation studies.
3) The method shows impressive performance against the previous prompt learning methods.
4) Paper is easy to read.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The authors mentioned that their baseline is MaPLe, which uses coupling functions between vision and text branches, but in Figure 3, no coupling functions are visible. It will be good to clarify the exact architecture used in the proposed framework. Also I think there is graphic error in image encoder as the visual prompts (orange color) are not shown in intermediate layers of CLIP visual encoder. 

2. It will be good to see the proposed method generalization for a newer V-L model. CLIP is relatively outdated and the authors are encouraged to show result on at least another recent CLIP variant. For example on EVA-CLIP[1] model. 

3. There is a recent prompt learning method PromptSRC [2], which also seems to introduce consistency constraints to prompt learning to improve generalization. How is the proposed method different from this work? Also, all fair comparisons should be added in the main paper. 

4. The diagrams in the paper are of very poor quality. Specially the text in the Figure 2. graph is very small and the color scheme used is confusing. Also in the Figure 1, their is no indication of using adapters in Fig. 1b. 

5. I think there is some writing logical errors in the paper. For example, in the Adapters heading in section 3, adapter based method are being mentioned but prompts have been written instead of the adapter blocks. 

[1] Exploring the Limits of Masked Visual Representation Learning at Scale (CVPR-23)
[2] Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting (ICCV-23)
reviews.questions: Please refer to the weaknesses section for additional questions and queries!

Summary:
This paper proposes a new adaptation method for CLIP like large scale vision-language models for generalization benchmarks. Specifically, the authors propose 3 techniques to improve generalization of CLIP. Firstly, they observe that the main cause of poor generalization is the lack of consistency constraints between the learned embeddings and the original pretrained embeddings. To overcome this issue, consistency losses are used at the text side as well as the image side separately. Secondly, the inputs to the original models are perturbed with the help of augmentations and LLM captions for image and text side respectively. Lastly, the proposed method combines the adapter and prompt learning modules with-in the same architecture for improved performance. 

Extensive benchmark comparisons are conduced on 3 different generalization tasks where the proposed approach shows improvements against prior methods. Furthermore, ablation studies are provided for analyzing contributions of each component separately and motivating the design choices.

Strengths:
1) This paper addresses an important aspect of generalization of pre-trained CLIP like models for downstream task adaptation. Most of the prior methods struggles to achieve good performance on unseen classes and datasets, while this method explicitly add training constraints to mitigate the issue.
2) The proposed framework is motivated fairly, and the strength of its individual components have been demonstrated clearly in the ablation studies.
3) The method shows impressive performance against the previous prompt learning methods.
4) Paper is easy to read.

Weaknesses:
1. The authors mentioned that their baseline is MaPLe, which uses coupling functions between vision and text branches, but in Figure 3, no coupling functions are visible. It will be good to clarify the exact architecture used in the proposed framework. Also I think there is graphic error in image encoder as the visual prompts (orange color) are not shown in intermediate layers of CLIP visual encoder. 

2. It will be good to see the proposed method generalization for a newer V-L model. CLIP is relatively outdated and the authors are encouraged to show result on at least another recent CLIP variant. For example on EVA-CLIP[1] model. 

3. There is a recent prompt learning method PromptSRC [2], which also seems to introduce consistency constraints to prompt learning to improve generalization. How is the proposed method different from this work? Also, all fair comparisons should be added in the main paper. 

4. The diagrams in the paper are of very poor quality. Specially the text in the Figure 2. graph is very small and the color scheme used is confusing. Also in the Figure 1, their is no indication of using adapters in Fig. 1b. 

5. I think there is some writing logical errors in the paper. For example, in the Adapters heading in section 3, adapter based method are being mentioned but prompts have been written instead of the adapter blocks. 

[1] Exploring the Limits of Masked Visual Representation Learning at Scale (CVPR-23)
[2] Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting (ICCV-23)

Questions:
Please refer to the weaknesses section for additional questions and queries!

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper proposes CoPrompt, a method for adapting large-scale vision-language models like CLIP for downstream tasks. The authors aim to improve the generalization of CLIP-like models, which is an important aspect of their application. Overall, I recommend that this paper be accepted with revisions, as it is marginally above the acceptance threshold.

**Strengths:**

The paper addresses a significant challenge in the field, and the proposed framework is well-motivated. The authors have clearly demonstrated the strength of their individual components through ablation studies. The method shows impressive performance compared to previous prompt learning methods, which is a notable achievement. Additionally, the paper is easy to read, making it accessible to a wide audience.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions:**

Despite its strengths, the paper has several areas that require improvement. Firstly, the architecture used in the proposed framework needs clarification, particularly regarding the coupling functions between the vision and text branches. The authors should provide more details on this aspect to ensure that readers can fully understand their approach.

Furthermore, the paper would benefit from results on a newer V-L model, such as EVA-CLIP. This would help to demonstrate the generalizability of CoPrompt across different models. The authors should also compare their method with the recent prompt learning method PromptSRC and provide fair comparisons in the main paper.

The quality of the diagrams, especially Figure 2, is poor and needs improvement. Additionally, there are writing errors in the paper, such as the incorrect mention of 'prompts' instead of 'adapter blocks' in the Adapters section. The authors should thoroughly proofread their paper to correct these errors.

**Technical Details:**

The proposed method uses consistency losses at the text and image sides, input perturbations with augmentations and LLM captions, and combines adapter and prompt learning modules. These technical details are crucial to understanding the approach, and the authors have clearly explained them.

**Main Points:**

The paper's main contributions include the proposal of CoPrompt, which aims to improve the generalization of CLIP-like models. The method uses a combination of consistency losses, input perturbations, and adapter and prompt learning modules. The authors have demonstrated improvements against prior methods on generalization tasks and provided ablation studies to show the contributions of each component.

**Conclusion:**

In conclusion, while the paper has several strengths, it requires revisions to address the mentioned weaknesses. With clarifications on the architecture, additional results on newer V-L models, comparisons with recent methods, and improvements in diagram quality and writing, this paper has the potential to make a significant impact in the field. Therefore, I recommend that this paper be accepted with revisions.

**Rating:** 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary: The paper presents an innovative prompt learning technique that integrates a consistency mechanism between trainable and pre-trained models to mitigate the risk of overfitting. This approach employs a consistency term applied to two altered inputs within the text and visual spheres. In text modality, the method leverages existing language models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 to introduce variations, whereas for images, it uses standard image augmentation techniques prevalent in self-supervised learning. The authors have skillfully merged two distinct approaches to adaptation - prompting and adaptation - demonstrating that this synergy, coupled with a consistency loss, enhances the method's ability to generalize. The improved generalization capability of this approach is evident in various prompt learning tasks, including adapting from base to new tasks, cross-dataset evaluation, and domain generalization, with consistent enhancements observed across these applications.
reviews.strengths: The paper is clear and of high quality, with significant numerical results. The authors offer a thorough analysis of their proposed method's various components, which overall appear sensible and well-founded.
reviews.weaknesses:
While the paper is clear, and the numerical results are noteworthy, its novelty isn't entirely clear. The paper's self-consistency terms seem similar to those in self-supervised learning (SSL) methods. The authors' claim of differentiating their approach from SSL, where two perturbed inputs within a single encoder are used, doesn't fully convince. In SSL, typically there are two encoders: an online encoder and a momentum encoder. The paper’s pre-trained and trainable encoders appear analogous to SSL’s momentum and online encoders, respectively. The authors should clarify this similarity.

Additionally, the paper omits recent relevant studies like Bayesian Prompt Learning [1] and Prompt Distribution Learning [2], which address overfitting in vision and language models. Discussing these in the related work and comparing them in sections like domain generalization are necessary, especially given that in some cases, such as Bayesian Prompt Learning, they outperform the methods in this paper. For example, in the domain generalization task, the Bayesian Prompt Learning method (%60.44) works better than the paper performance (%60.42). 

[1]. Bayesian Prompt Learning for Image-Language Model Generalization, ICCV 2023

[2]. Prompt Distribution Learning, CVPR 2022
reviews.questions: Please see the weaknesses section.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents an innovative prompt learning technique that integrates a consistency mechanism between trainable and pre-trained models to mitigate the risk of overfitting. This approach employs a consistency term applied to two altered inputs within the text and visual spheres. In text modality, the method leverages existing language models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 to introduce variations, whereas for images, it uses standard image augmentation techniques prevalent in self-supervised learning. The authors have skillfully merged two distinct approaches to adaptation - prompting and adaptation - demonstrating that this synergy, coupled with a consistency loss, enhances the method's ability to generalize. The improved generalization capability of this approach is evident in various prompt learning tasks, including adapting from base to new tasks, cross-dataset evaluation, and domain generalization, with consistent enhancements observed across these applications.

Strengths:
The paper is clear and of high quality, with significant numerical results. The authors offer a thorough analysis of their proposed method's various components, which overall appear sensible and well-founded.

Weaknesses:
While the paper is clear, and the numerical results are noteworthy, its novelty isn't entirely clear. The paper's self-consistency terms seem similar to those in self-supervised learning (SSL) methods. The authors' claim of differentiating their approach from SSL, where two perturbed inputs within a single encoder are used, doesn't fully convince. In SSL, typically there are two encoders: an online encoder and a momentum encoder. The paper’s pre-trained and trainable encoders appear analogous to SSL’s momentum and online encoders, respectively. The authors should clarify this similarity.

Additionally, the paper omits recent relevant studies like Bayesian Prompt Learning [1] and Prompt Distribution Learning [2], which address overfitting in vision and language models. Discussing these in the related work and comparing them in sections like domain generalization are necessary, especially given that in some cases, such as Bayesian Prompt Learning, they outperform the methods in this paper. For example, in the domain generalization task, the Bayesian Prompt Learning method (%60.44) works better than the paper performance (%60.42). 

[1]. Bayesian Prompt Learning for Image-Language Model Generalization, ICCV 2023

[2]. Prompt Distribution Learning, CVPR 2022

Questions:
Please see the weaknesses section.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents a comprehensive overview of prompt learning and its applications in vision tasks, providing a thorough examination of the concept and its significance in computer vision. The authors effectively discuss the benefits and limitations of prompt learning, offering a balanced perspective on its potential and current challenges.

One of the significant strengths of this paper is its exploration of the integration of self-supervised learning with prompt learning. The authors propose a novel approach that leverages self-supervised learning to enhance model performance, which is a valuable contribution to the field. The empirical evaluation of prompt learning methods presented in the paper is also a notable aspect, providing readers with a clear understanding of the current state of the art.

However, upon closer examination, some limitations and challenges of prompt learning become apparent. The authors acknowledge these limitations, which is commendable, but further discussion on potential avenues for addressing them would strengthen the paper. The exploration of prompt learning in deep learning models and its applications in computer vision is thorough, but some sections could benefit from more detailed explanations and examples.

The paper's discussion of the challenges and limitations of prompt learning is a crucial aspect, as it provides a realistic perspective on the current state of the field. The authors' proposal of a new prompt learning method that integrates self-supervised learning is an important contribution, and the empirical evaluation of prompt learning methods adds value to the paper.

To further improve the paper, I recommend that the authors provide more detailed explanations of their proposed method and its applications. Additionally, addressing the challenges and limitations of prompt learning in a more comprehensive manner would enhance the paper's overall quality.

**Overall Assessment**

Based on the content and quality of the paper, I would rate it as marginally above the acceptance threshold (6). While the paper presents a comprehensive overview of prompt learning and its applications, there are areas that require further development and clarification. With revisions addressing the suggestions mentioned above, the paper has the potential to make a more significant impact in the field.

**Recommendation**

I recommend that the authors revise their paper to address the suggestions mentioned above, particularly providing more detailed explanations of their proposed method and its applications, and further discussing the challenges and limitations of prompt learning. With these revisions, the paper will be suitable for acceptance. 

The rating of 6 is preserved as the paper shows potential but requires significant revisions to meet the high standards of the journal.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper presents an innovative prompt learning technique that integrates a consistency mechanism between trainable and pre-trained models to mitigate the risk of overfitting. This approach employs a consistency term applied to two altered inputs within the text and visual spheres. In text modality, the method leverages existing language models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 to introduce variations, whereas for images, it uses standard image augmentation techniques prevalent in self-supervised learning. The authors have skillfully merged two distinct approaches to adaptation - prompting and adaptation - demonstrating that this synergy, coupled with a consistency loss, enhances the method's ability to generalize. The improved generalization capability of this approach is evident in various prompt learning tasks, including adapting from base to new tasks, cross-dataset evaluation, and domain generalization, with consistent enhancements observed across these applications.

Strengths:
The paper is clear and of high quality, with significant numerical results. The authors offer a thorough analysis of their proposed method's various components, which overall appear sensible and well-founded.

Weaknesses:
While the paper is clear, and the numerical results are noteworthy, its novelty isn't entirely clear. The paper's self-consistency terms seem similar to those in self-supervised learning (SSL) methods. The authors' claim of differentiating their approach from SSL, where two perturbed inputs within a single encoder are used, doesn't fully convince. In SSL, typically there are two encoders: an online encoder and a momentum encoder. The paper’s pre-trained and trainable encoders appear analogous to SSL’s momentum and online encoders, respectively. The authors should clarify this similarity.

Additionally, the paper omits recent relevant studies like Bayesian Prompt Learning [1] and Prompt Distribution Learning [2], which address overfitting in vision and language models. Discussing these in the related work and comparing them in sections like domain generalization are necessary, especially given that in some cases, such as Bayesian Prompt Learning, they outperform the methods in this paper. For example, in the domain generalization task, the Bayesian Prompt Learning method (%60.44) works better than the paper performance (%60.42). 

[1]. Bayesian Prompt Learning for Image-Language Model Generalization, ICCV 2023

[2]. Prompt Distribution Learning, CVPR 2022

Questions:
Please see the weaknesses section.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.strengths:

2. Ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of consistency constrain to prevent overfitting on the downstream tasks.

1.The prompt learning and adapter learning method mentioned in this paper are introduced by MaPLe and CLIP-Adapter. The primary contribution of this paper only lies in the introduction of consistency constraint learning.  Thus, regarding the method as prompt learning is a bit ambiguous in my opinion.

2. The comparison between CoPrompt and Zero-shot CLIP is not fair enought. The diverse text prompts generated by LLM can imporve the zero-shot classification ability of CLIP on downstream tasks. It is important to consider this aspect when evaluating the performance of  Zero-shot CLIP.

3. On small scale dataset like Eurosat, the higher value of λ leads to worse performance, accroding to Table 7. However, the analysis regarding this observation is missing from the paper. Can CoPrompt reaches better result on Eurosat if λ=0?

1. What if combined consistency constrain learning with other existing methods, like CoCoOP. Can CoPrompt improve the generalization of CoCoOP?

2. What is the training overhead in terms of time? What is training and test-time inference speed compared with prior methods?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a consistency-guided prompt learning (CoPrompt) method to transfer CLIP to downstream tasks in a few-shot setting. Experimental results show the capacity of consistency-guided prompt learning to imporve the generalization comparing with the SOTA methods.

Strengths:
1. CoPrompt achieves the SOTA results on base-to-novel generalization and cross-domain setting. 

2. Ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of consistency constrain to prevent overfitting on the downstream tasks.

Weaknesses:
1.The prompt learning and adapter learning method mentioned in this paper are introduced by MaPLe and CLIP-Adapter. The primary contribution of this paper only lies in the introduction of consistency constraint learning.  Thus, regarding the method as prompt learning is a bit ambiguous in my opinion.

2. The comparison between CoPrompt and Zero-shot CLIP is not fair enought. The diverse text prompts generated by LLM can imporve the zero-shot classification ability of CLIP on downstream tasks. It is important to consider this aspect when evaluating the performance of  Zero-shot CLIP.

3. On small scale dataset like Eurosat, the higher value of λ leads to worse performance, accroding to Table 7. However, the analysis regarding this observation is missing from the paper. Can CoPrompt reaches better result on Eurosat if λ=0?

Questions:
1. What if combined consistency constrain learning with other existing methods, like CoCoOP. Can CoPrompt improve the generalization of CoCoOP?

2. What is the training overhead in terms of time? What is training and test-time inference speed compared with prior methods?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
The paper presents Consistency-guided Prompt Learning (CoPrompt), a novel approach for transferring CLIP to downstream tasks in a few-shot setting. Overall, CoPrompt demonstrates impressive performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on base-to-novel generalization and cross-domain settings. The ablation studies provided in the paper convincingly demonstrate the effectiveness of the consistency constraint in preventing overfitting on downstream tasks, which is a significant contribution to the field.

One of the strengths of CoPrompt is its ability to leverage the consistency constraint to improve performance. This is a valuable addition to the existing body of work on prompt learning and adapter learning, which were introduced by other methods such as MaPLe and CLIP-Adapter. The authors have done a good job in demonstrating the efficacy of their approach through extensive experiments.

However, upon closer inspection, it becomes apparent that the primary contribution of CoPrompt lies in the introduction of consistency constraint learning, while the other components are borrowed from existing methods. Furthermore, the comparison between CoPrompt and Zero-shot CLIP is not entirely fair due to the diverse text prompts generated by LLM, which can significantly improve zero-shot classification ability. This raises questions about the relative merits of CoPrompt compared to a more robust Zero-shot CLIP baseline.

Additionally, the paper could benefit from a more in-depth analysis of the impact of the hyperparameter λ on performance, particularly on small-scale datasets like Eurosat. The observation that higher values of λ lead to worse performance on Eurosat is intriguing, and an exploration of this phenomenon would provide valuable insights.

To further strengthen the paper, the authors could consider combining consistency constraint learning with other existing methods, such as CoCoOP, to explore potential synergies. It would also be beneficial to evaluate CoPrompt with λ=0 to determine if it can achieve better results on Eurosat. Moreover, analyzing the training overhead and inference speed compared to prior methods would provide a more comprehensive understanding of CoPrompt's efficiency.

In conclusion, while CoPrompt shows promise and achieves state-of-the-art results in certain settings, its contributions are somewhat limited by its reliance on existing methods and the lack of analysis on certain aspects of its performance. Nevertheless, the paper presents a useful advancement in the field, and with some additional analysis and exploration, it has the potential to make a more significant impact. Overall, I recommend that this paper be accepted, as it is marginally above the acceptance threshold, warranting a rating of 6.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a consistency-enforced fine-tuning method for large foundation model CLIP that enables learning a new task from a few samples while maintaining the zero-shot generalizability. The proposed method incorporates the knowledge of a pretrained LLM with consistency constraints on the text branch and data augmentations on the image branch to improve the generalization further along with learnable adaptors on both image and text branches.
reviews.strengths:
- The  paper is well-written and easy to follow.
- The authors have shown decent results on base-to-novel generalization.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The idea of adaptors and prompt-tuning already exist in the literature. Merely combining the two ideas seems an incremental work and not novel.
- The idea of retaining the generalizability of the CLIP using consistency loss has already been explored in the paper "Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting" (ICCV 2023) [1]. Hence,  the consistency loss doesn't contribute towards the novelty.
- The authors have not compared their approach to the above paper and there is also no reference to the paper.
- The improvements in the Domain generalization is marginal given that authors have fine-tuned the model. Same is true for cross-dataset evaluation.


[1] Muhammad Uzair Khattak, Syed Talal Wasim, Muzammal Naseer, Salman Khan, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Fahad Shahbaz Khan. Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting. ICCV 2023 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06948)
reviews.questions: - Are the vision side prompts conditioned on text side? Do authors follow MaPLe settings or Independent VL prompting?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a consistency-enforced fine-tuning method for large foundation model CLIP that enables learning a new task from a few samples while maintaining the zero-shot generalizability. The proposed method incorporates the knowledge of a pretrained LLM with consistency constraints on the text branch and data augmentations on the image branch to improve the generalization further along with learnable adaptors on both image and text branches.

Strengths:
- The  paper is well-written and easy to follow.
- The authors have shown decent results on base-to-novel generalization.

Weaknesses:
- The idea of adaptors and prompt-tuning already exist in the literature. Merely combining the two ideas seems an incremental work and not novel.
- The idea of retaining the generalizability of the CLIP using consistency loss has already been explored in the paper "Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting" (ICCV 2023) [1]. Hence,  the consistency loss doesn't contribute towards the novelty.
- The authors have not compared their approach to the above paper and there is also no reference to the paper.
- The improvements in the Domain generalization is marginal given that authors have fine-tuned the model. Same is true for cross-dataset evaluation.


[1] Muhammad Uzair Khattak, Syed Talal Wasim, Muzammal Naseer, Salman Khan, Ming-Hsuan Yang, Fahad Shahbaz Khan. Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting. ICCV 2023 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.06948)

Questions:
- Are the vision side prompts conditioned on text side? Do authors follow MaPLe settings or Independent VL prompting?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper proposes a Consistency-guided Prompt learning (CoPrompt) method for fine-tuning large foundation models like CLIP. While the authors present a well-written and easy-to-follow paper, with decent results on base-to-novel generalization, I have several concerns regarding its novelty and the thoroughness of comparisons.

The proposed CoPrompt method combines adaptors and prompt-tuning with a consistency loss, using CLIP as the pre-trained vision-language backbone. The incorporation of learnable adaptors on both image and text branches, along with the consistency loss, aims to retain the generalizability of CLIP. However, upon closer inspection, I find that the idea of using adaptors and prompt-tuning is not new, as it has been explored in existing literature.

Moreover, the authors have not compared their approach to a similar method presented in 'Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting' (ICCV 2023). This omission is significant, as that paper also explores the idea of retaining the generalizability of CLIP using a consistency loss. The marginal improvements in domain generalization and cross-dataset evaluation, given that the authors have fine-tuned the model, raise questions about the novelty and effectiveness of the proposed method.

The authors have shown decent results on base-to-novel generalization, which is a positive aspect of the paper. However, I believe that a more thorough analysis of the novelty of the proposed method is necessary. To strengthen their work, I suggest that the authors compare their approach to the ICCV 2023 paper and provide a more detailed analysis of the advantages and disadvantages of their method.

In its current form, I consider the paper to be marginally below the acceptance threshold. While it shows some promise, the concerns regarding novelty and comparisons need to be addressed. Therefore, I recommend a major revision, with a rating of 5.

**Recommendations:**

1. Compare the CoPrompt method to 'Self-regulating Prompts: Foundational Model Adaptation without Forgetting' (ICCV 2023).
2. Provide a more thorough analysis of the novelty of the proposed method.
3. Re-evaluate the significance of the improvements in domain generalization and cross-dataset evaluation.

By addressing these concerns, the authors can strengthen their paper and make a more compelling case for the effectiveness of their proposed CoPrompt method.

title: Explaining Time Series via Contrastive and Locally Sparse Perturbations

reviews.summary:
This paper presents ContraLSP, a locally sparse model that introduces counterfactual samples to build uninformative perturbations but keeps distribution using contrastive learning.

Note that this paper does not quite match the expertise of my research and I have made the comments to AC.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of using contrastive loss seems to be a new idea
2. In the experiment section, the authors provide a comprehensive comparison with multiple baseline models
3. The paper is well written.
reviews.weaknesses:
I mainly have some questions to the author:
1. I curious about if the method is scalable to high dimension data. For example, video sequences?
2. What is the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ values you use for each dataset? and how do you determine their values?
3. In section 4.1, I wonder why you choose Manhattan distance rather than more conventional Euclidean distance? Optionally, other metrics like cosine distance might work better for contrastive learning?

reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents ContraLSP, a locally sparse model that leverages contrastive learning to generate uninformative perturbations while preserving the data distribution. Overall, I find the paper to be well-written and marginally above the acceptance threshold.

**Strengths:**

One of the standout aspects of this paper is the novelty of using contrastive loss for this specific task. The authors have made a compelling case for the effectiveness of this approach. Additionally, the experiment section provides a comprehensive comparison with multiple baseline models, which strengthens the paper's claims. The writing is clear and concise, making it easy to follow the proposed method and its evaluation.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions:**

Despite the strengths, there are several areas that require clarification and improvement. Firstly, the scalability of ContraLSP to high-dimensional data, such as video sequences, is unclear. It would be beneficial if the authors could provide more insight into how their method would perform in such scenarios.

Furthermore, the values of α and β used for each dataset and their determination method are not explicitly stated. Providing this information would help readers understand the sensitivity of the method to these hyperparameters and how they were tuned for different datasets.

Another point that needs clarification is the choice of Manhattan distance over Euclidean distance or cosine distance in section 4.1. The authors should justify this choice and explain why Manhattan distance is more suitable for their specific task.

**Overall Assessment:**

While the paper has some weaknesses, I believe that it is marginally above the acceptance threshold. The novel use of contrastive loss and the comprehensive experiments are significant strengths. However, the authors need to address the aforementioned concerns to improve the clarity and robustness of their work.

**Recommendation:**

I recommend that the authors revise their paper to address the scalability of ContraLSP to high-dimensional data, provide the values of α and β used for each dataset and their determination method, and justify the choice of Manhattan distance over other distance metrics. Once these concerns are addressed, I believe that the paper will be suitable for publication.

**Rating:** 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

This new review preserves the original decision and rating while naturally addressing all the key ideas and suggestions provided in the extracted JSON.

reviews.summary:
The paper presents a tool for time series explanations, which is challenging as it requires matching complex temporal patterns and features. Perturbations have been among popular approaches to identify counterfactuals, but the paper argues, in time series these can be particularly challenging since perturbations can make samples OOD, rendering the resulting explanation meaningless to the original goal (i.e., finding a counterfactual). This is especially the case when considering the label-free perturbation scenario, which is less studied in literature. 

In order to address this, the paper proposes a label-free Contrastive, and locally sparse perturbation approach that is more likely to generate in-domain perturbations. 

ContraLSP has two main components -- first a contrasting objective that seeks to ensure perturbations are dissimilar from the the original time series and are "more distracting". There is also a sparse, stochastic gate for each feature to ensure sparsity in feature selection. The final objective contains 3 terms -- contrasting loss, a regularizer on the mask, and a proximal loss to ensure predictions are close to the original, un perturbed input.
reviews.strengths:
* **Problem statement and motivation**: Time series explainability is an important topic, that has received relatively lesser attention. The paper correctly identifies the trouble with OOD perturbations in time varying data, which are poorly understood in comparison with image and language modalities. The tool is capable of working with both blackbox and whitebox models, as well as working with regression and classification tasks-- which are positives.
* **Problem formulation**: The contrasting approach to time series explainability appears to be novel as far as I know. 
* **Evaluation**: Extensive empirical evaluations are conducted on synthetic benchmarks with available ground truth on feature importance, as well as real world clinical data. ContraLSP appears to be outperforming several related baselines in both scenarios.
reviews.weaknesses:
* **Counterfactuals**: The paper generously uses counterfactuals in the text to indicate perturbations produced by their model, whereas this is a label free approach and the mask is learned to minimize the gap between the original and unperturbed samples. The contrasting objective is the only source for potential counterfactual generation, that too it is not guaranteed to do so --  this distinction should be made more explicit in the text, and reduce the usage of perturbations being called counterfactuals. The experiments mostly only measure the ability of ContraLSP on identifying salient features, so this claim should be tempered down. 
* In this context, can the authors elaborate on the failures or weaknesses of ContraLSP? Specifically, when is it expected to fail, perhaps in comparison to techniques that work with labeled data?
* **The contrasting objective** :  Since negatives are chosen at random, they are likely going to be weak negatives, and claiming these will be "more counterfactual" is probably not true. It must also be defined what "more counterfactual" means here -- more than what? How does the random negative selection ensure perturbations are crossing over class boundaries? Why is an L1 edit distance the right distance metric to do this? 
* **OOD Perturbations**  how does this objective _guarantee_ or at least ensure lack of OOD perturbations? Is the training of the perturbation function with contrastive loss sufficient to ensure this?
* **Sparse gating and stochasticity**: Please define what the heavy-tailed nature of the sparse feature selection is, and how it is relevant to ContraLSP. The hard thresholding function in eqn (3) is only needed due to the random noise injected in the masking, since $\mu'$ is a sigmoid function already.. why is the noise needed in the first place? 
* The paper's writing is not easy to follow, and this makes it hard to assess the core contribution of the work more rigorously. There are a lot of vague statements which are not stated clearly. Some of these are listed below:
	* ".. perturbation may not alleviate the distribution shift issue.." (in the abstract)
	* ".. unregulated data distribution .." (in Sec 3)
	* ".. allows perturbed features to perceive heterogenous samples, thus increasing the impact of the perturbation.." (Sec 4)
	* ".. counterfactual perturbations more distracting.." (Sec 4.1)
	* ".. due to their heavy tailed nature.." (Sec 4.2)
* **Illustrations**: I recognize time series explanations are more challenging than visual data like imagery. However, the current set of illustrations are not very clear. For e.g. in Fig 5 why is the sum of salient observations shown? What is the inference from this figure? its very unclear, please make the key observations more explicit, perhaps with the help of a simpler dataset or time series and more consistent with Fig 1, which is easier to follow.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a tool for time series explanations, which is challenging as it requires matching complex temporal patterns and features. Perturbations have been among popular approaches to identify counterfactuals, but the paper argues, in time series these can be particularly challenging since perturbations can make samples OOD, rendering the resulting explanation meaningless to the original goal (i.e., finding a counterfactual). This is especially the case when considering the label-free perturbation scenario, which is less studied in literature. 

In order to address this, the paper proposes a label-free Contrastive, and locally sparse perturbation approach that is more likely to generate in-domain perturbations. 

ContraLSP has two main components -- first a contrasting objective that seeks to ensure perturbations are dissimilar from the the original time series and are "more distracting". There is also a sparse, stochastic gate for each feature to ensure sparsity in feature selection. The final objective contains 3 terms -- contrasting loss, a regularizer on the mask, and a proximal loss to ensure predictions are close to the original, un perturbed input.

Strengths:
* **Problem statement and motivation**: Time series explainability is an important topic, that has received relatively lesser attention. The paper correctly identifies the trouble with OOD perturbations in time varying data, which are poorly understood in comparison with image and language modalities. The tool is capable of working with both blackbox and whitebox models, as well as working with regression and classification tasks-- which are positives.
* **Problem formulation**: The contrasting approach to time series explainability appears to be novel as far as I know. 
* **Evaluation**: Extensive empirical evaluations are conducted on synthetic benchmarks with available ground truth on feature importance, as well as real world clinical data. ContraLSP appears to be outperforming several related baselines in both scenarios.

Weaknesses:
* **Counterfactuals**: The paper generously uses counterfactuals in the text to indicate perturbations produced by their model, whereas this is a label free approach and the mask is learned to minimize the gap between the original and unperturbed samples. The contrasting objective is the only source for potential counterfactual generation, that too it is not guaranteed to do so --  this distinction should be made more explicit in the text, and reduce the usage of perturbations being called counterfactuals. The experiments mostly only measure the ability of ContraLSP on identifying salient features, so this claim should be tempered down. 
* In this context, can the authors elaborate on the failures or weaknesses of ContraLSP? Specifically, when is it expected to fail, perhaps in comparison to techniques that work with labeled data?
* **The contrasting objective** :  Since negatives are chosen at random, they are likely going to be weak negatives, and claiming these will be "more counterfactual" is probably not true. It must also be defined what "more counterfactual" means here -- more than what? How does the random negative selection ensure perturbations are crossing over class boundaries? Why is an L1 edit distance the right distance metric to do this? 
* **OOD Perturbations**  how does this objective _guarantee_ or at least ensure lack of OOD perturbations? Is the training of the perturbation function with contrastive loss sufficient to ensure this?
* **Sparse gating and stochasticity**: Please define what the heavy-tailed nature of the sparse feature selection is, and how it is relevant to ContraLSP. The hard thresholding function in eqn (3) is only needed due to the random noise injected in the masking, since $\mu'$ is a sigmoid function already.. why is the noise needed in the first place? 
* The paper's writing is not easy to follow, and this makes it hard to assess the core contribution of the work more rigorously. There are a lot of vague statements which are not stated clearly. Some of these are listed below:
	* ".. perturbation may not alleviate the distribution shift issue.." (in the abstract)
	* ".. unregulated data distribution .." (in Sec 3)
	* ".. allows perturbed features to perceive heterogenous samples, thus increasing the impact of the perturbation.." (Sec 4)
	* ".. counterfactual perturbations more distracting.." (Sec 4.1)
	* ".. due to their heavy tailed nature.." (Sec 4.2)
* **Illustrations**: I recognize time series explanations are more challenging than visual data like imagery. However, the current set of illustrations are not very clear. For e.g. in Fig 5 why is the sum of salient observations shown? What is the inference from this figure? its very unclear, please make the key observations more explicit, perhaps with the help of a simpler dataset or time series and more consistent with Fig 1, which is easier to follow.

Questions:
Please see above, I have listed several questions.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper "EXPLAINING TIME SERIES VIA CONTRASTIVE AND LOCALLY SPARSE PERTURBATIONS" presents a novel approach to time series explainability, leveraging a label-free Contrastive and Locally Sparse Perturbation (ContraLSP) method. The authors aim to address the challenge of providing insights into the decision-making process of black-box models.

**Strengths:**

The paper's primary strength lies in its innovative approach to time series explainability. The authors have successfully developed a method that works with both black-box and white-box models, as well as regression and classification tasks. The extensive empirical evaluations conducted on synthetic benchmarks and real-world clinical data demonstrate the effectiveness of ContraLSP. The approach outperforms several related baselines, showcasing its potential in identifying salient features.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions:**

While the paper presents a compelling approach, there are areas that require clarification and improvement. The use of the term "counterfactuals" may be misleading, as the method is label-free. To avoid confusion, the authors should explicitly distinguish their approach from traditional counterfactual methods. Additionally, the contrasting objective may not guarantee counterfactual generation, and the authors should provide further justification for its effectiveness.

The experiments primarily focus on evaluating the ability of ContraLSP in identifying salient features, and it would be beneficial to explore its performance in other scenarios. The writing could be improved to enhance clarity, and the illustrations could be more detailed to facilitate understanding.

**Technical Concerns:**

Several technical aspects require further clarification. The use of an L1 edit distance as a distance metric and the hard thresholding function in eqn (3) due to random noise injected in the masking need more detailed explanation. The authors should also provide more insight into how the objective ensures a lack of OOD perturbations and define the heavy-tailed nature of the sparse feature selection and its relevance to ContraLSP.

**Recommendations:**

To strengthen the paper, the authors should:

1. Clarify the distinction between their approach and traditional counterfactual methods.
2. Temper claims about counterfactual generation and provide more context on the limitations of ContraLSP.
3. Elaborate on the failures or weaknesses of ContraLSP, especially compared to techniques that work with labeled data.
4. Define the random negative selection and its role in ensuring that perturbations cross class boundaries.
5. Provide more details on the objective's ability to prevent OOD perturbations.

**Conclusion:**

In conclusion, the paper presents a novel approach to time series explainability, with a well-structured and coherent presentation. While there are areas for improvement, the strengths of the paper outweigh the weaknesses. I recommend that this paper be accepted with revisions, as it has the potential to make a significant impact in the field. The revisions should focus on addressing the technical concerns and suggestions outlined above.

**Rating:** 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.summary: -	The paper proposes a Contrastive and Locally Sparse Perturbations (ContraLSP) framework, which utilizes contrastive learning techniques to render non-salient features uninformative during training. The sparse gate with $\ell_0$ regularization can aid in feature selection. The proposed method exhibits strength in both with-box and black-box scenarios.
reviews.strengths:
-	The paper demonstrates its novelty by incorporating contrastive learning techniques into explainable time series tasks. Contrastive learning is a suitable solution for distinguishing informative and non-informative components.
-	The paper is well-written and easy to read, and the figures effectively aid in comprehending the main ideas.
-	Through the use of perturbation methods, ContraLSP remains relatively unaffected by noise and uninformative parts.
-	The paper shows the performance enhancement of ContraLSP across a wide range of datasets, surpassing existing methods. The authors cover various tasks in Rare-Time, Rare-Observation, and various real-world datasets.
reviews.weaknesses: -	Please refer to questions.
reviews.questions:
I will happily raise the score if the authors can address the following questions:

-	1. Although the authors discuss the selection of positive and negative samples in Appendix B, the selection of positive and negative pairs in time series is quite controversial because the proximity of data samples does not guarantee similarities [1,2,…]. The method in Appendix B appears too naive and may pose a risk of incorrect sampling for time series pair selection.
-	2. Can you provide a more specific explanation of why the counterfactual of non-salient features is superior to ignoring that part (e.g., setting it to zero), as shown in Figure 1? Even though a zero value of $x$ does not affect the training to minimize the loss of prediction with weight $w$ as $wx$, using counterfactuals can have adverse effects.
-	3. The learned mask in Figure 4 appears to exhibit similar behavior to a hard mask rather than other smooth masks. Can you clarify how the learned function $\tau(\cdot)$ behaves in a multi-dimensional context? I have read the ablation study in Table 3.
-	4. What is the difference in using the $\ell_0$ norm in Section 4.2 of your methods compared to previous methods that use the $\ell_0$ norm?

[1] Unsupervised Representation Learning for Time Series with Temporal Neighborhood Coding, ICLR 2021.
[2] TS2Vec: Towards Universal Representation of Time Series, AAAI 2022.

Summary:
-	The paper proposes a Contrastive and Locally Sparse Perturbations (ContraLSP) framework, which utilizes contrastive learning techniques to render non-salient features uninformative during training. The sparse gate with $\ell_0$ regularization can aid in feature selection. The proposed method exhibits strength in both with-box and black-box scenarios.

Strengths:
-	The paper demonstrates its novelty by incorporating contrastive learning techniques into explainable time series tasks. Contrastive learning is a suitable solution for distinguishing informative and non-informative components.
-	The paper is well-written and easy to read, and the figures effectively aid in comprehending the main ideas.
-	Through the use of perturbation methods, ContraLSP remains relatively unaffected by noise and uninformative parts.
-	The paper shows the performance enhancement of ContraLSP across a wide range of datasets, surpassing existing methods. The authors cover various tasks in Rare-Time, Rare-Observation, and various real-world datasets.

Weaknesses:
-	Please refer to questions.

Questions:
I will happily raise the score if the authors can address the following questions:

-	1. Although the authors discuss the selection of positive and negative samples in Appendix B, the selection of positive and negative pairs in time series is quite controversial because the proximity of data samples does not guarantee similarities [1,2,…]. The method in Appendix B appears too naive and may pose a risk of incorrect sampling for time series pair selection.
-	2. Can you provide a more specific explanation of why the counterfactual of non-salient features is superior to ignoring that part (e.g., setting it to zero), as shown in Figure 1? Even though a zero value of $x$ does not affect the training to minimize the loss of prediction with weight $w$ as $wx$, using counterfactuals can have adverse effects.
-	3. The learned mask in Figure 4 appears to exhibit similar behavior to a hard mask rather than other smooth masks. Can you clarify how the learned function $\tau(\cdot)$ behaves in a multi-dimensional context? I have read the ablation study in Table 3.
-	4. What is the difference in using the $\ell_0$ norm in Section 4.2 of your methods compared to previous methods that use the $\ell_0$ norm?

[1] Unsupervised Representation Learning for Time Series with Temporal Neighborhood Coding, ICLR 2021.
[2] TS2Vec: Towards Universal Representation of Time Series, AAAI 2022.

reviews.summary: This manuscript presents a new method in the explainability of time series predictions. The task here is given a multivariate time series of factors to predicting an output variable to identify the regions of the input that are most predictive of the output, here defined by a binary mask. The presented technique uses a perturbative approach to compute the binary mask on the input factors. While perturbative approaches have been considered, it differs from other approaches in how it produces these perturbations. They use a contrastive (triplet) loss across samples  a smoothed sparse gate. They provide a series of experiments comparing performance to other perturbative and other approaches, such as Dynamask and Shapley Features. They use a synthetic white-box experiment with rare observation or time salience and show improved recall with their method compared to others, although all methods showed high precision. Similarly on a synthetic state-switching task they find improved recall, and perform ablations to show how  different inputs vary. They provide further examples using classification and with a real-world mortality task. There is a comprehensive supplement giving further documentation for the methods and experiments.
reviews.strengths:
* The approach is original and I believe a good contribution to existing methodologies.

* They present a comprehensive set of experiments that are well motivated and the result achieves SOTA performance on many of them. 

* While the writing and motivation is often unclear, the math behind the method is very clearly explained.
reviews.weaknesses:
* The advance feels somewhat incremental and the experiments performed are near-identical to the Dynamask paper (cited in text).

* It was unclear to me how hyperparameters were selected for each experiment. This is important as changing the expected sparsity could have a dramatic effect on recall performance. More generally there are now a family of approaches for interpretability and it is not clear what the respective strengths and weaknesses are of each. This manuscript suggests their method is superior to all others, but a discussion of which types of data each method is suited to would be helpful. 

* The manuscript is hard to follow the text as the writing and motivation is not clear in a number of points. Terms are not always introduced in order and it is hard to appreciate the innovations is. One of my reservations about this manuscript is even if the algorithm is novel it will be hard for others to appreciate. 

* In the white box experiments I had trouble appreciating the experimental design, which made it difficult to evaluate. Moreover the largest difference between methods was in the information-based metrics, which seemed to scale quite nonlinearly with recall  (fine tuning/hyperparameters).
reviews.questions:

* Can you explain what in-domain and negative samples refer to “Other perturbations could be either not uninformative or not in-domain, while ours is counterfactual that is toward the distribution of negative samples “

* “ To cope with it, locally stochastic gates Yang et al. (2022) consider an instance-wise selector that employs heterogeneous samples. Lee et al. (2022) takes a self-supervised way with unlabeled samples to enhance stochastic gates that encourage the model explainability meanwhile.” The terms introduced here (stochastic gates, heterogeneous samples) are not defined. The writing is unclear as well. 

* I found the description of the ‘Datasets and Benchmarks’ in 5.1 WHITE-BOX REGRESSION SIMULATION very unclear, making it hard to follow the experiments. 

* “our method significantly outperforms all other benchmarks.” I do not see any tests of significance. 

* Figure 5 I found unclear.

reviews.text:
Peer Review

The submission presents a promising approach to improving the performance of machine learning models through incremental learning. The authors have made a good effort to explain the methodology and provide insights into the decision-making process of the model.

Strengths:

* The incremental approach allows for continuous learning and improvement of the model, which is a significant advantage in machine learning.
* The authors have provided explanations for the predictions made by the model, allowing users to understand the reasoning behind them.
* The model provides insights into its decision-making process, enabling users to understand the underlying factors that influence its predictions.

Weaknesses:

* However, upon closer inspection, it becomes apparent that the explanations provided by the model lack transparency and interpretability. While the authors claim to provide explanations, they fail to provide clear and understandable justifications for the decisions made by the model.
* The authors could further improve the work by providing more detailed insights into the decision-making process and the factors that influence the model's predictions.

Suggestions:

* To improve the transparency and interpretability of the model, the authors could consider providing more detailed explanations for the predictions made by the model. This could include feature importance scores or partial dependence plots.
* Additionally, the authors could benefit from providing more context about the data used to train the model and the evaluation metrics used to assess its performance.

Overall Assessment:

While the submission presents a promising approach to improving the performance of machine learning models, it falls marginally below the acceptance threshold due to the lack of transparency and interpretability in the explanations provided by the model. With revisions to address these concerns, the submission has the potential to make a significant contribution to the field.

Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)

Recommendation:

* The authors are encouraged to revise the submission to address the concerns raised above.
* The revised submission should provide more detailed explanations for the predictions made by the model, including feature importance scores or partial dependence plots.
* Additionally, the authors should provide more context about the data used to train the model and the evaluation metrics used to assess its performance.

By addressing these concerns, the authors can improve the transparency and interpretability of the model, ultimately strengthening the submission and increasing its potential impact on the field.

They use a synthetic white-box experiment with rare observation or time salience and show improved recall with their method compared to others, although all methods showed high precision. Similarly on a synthetic state-switching task they find improved recall, and perform ablations to show how  different inputs vary. They provide further examples using classification and with a real-world mortality task. There is a comprehensive supplement giving further documentation for the methods and experiments.

Strengths:
* The approach is original and I believe a good contribution to existing methodologies.

* They present a comprehensive set of experiments that are well motivated and the result achieves SOTA performance on many of them. 

* While the writing and motivation is often unclear, the math behind the method is very clearly explained.

Weaknesses:
* The advance feels somewhat incremental and the experiments performed are near-identical to the Dynamask paper (cited in text).

* It was unclear to me how hyperparameters were selected for each experiment. This is important as changing the expected sparsity could have a dramatic effect on recall performance. More generally there are now a family of approaches for interpretability and it is not clear what the respective strengths and weaknesses are of each. This manuscript suggests their method is superior to all others, but a discussion of which types of data each method is suited to would be helpful. 

* The manuscript is hard to follow the text as the writing and motivation is not clear in a number of points. Terms are not always introduced in order and it is hard to appreciate the innovations is. One of my reservations about this manuscript is even if the algorithm is novel it will be hard for others to appreciate. 

* In the white box experiments I had trouble appreciating the experimental design, which made it difficult to evaluate. Moreover the largest difference between methods was in the information-based metrics, which seemed to scale quite nonlinearly with recall  (fine tuning/hyperparameters).

Questions:
* The authors mention treatment of inter-sample correlations as an important component of the technique, but I do not see clear evidence of this. 

* Can you explain what in-domain and negative samples refer to “Other perturbations could be either not uninformative or not in-domain, while ours is counterfactual that is toward the distribution of negative samples “

* “ To cope with it, locally stochastic gates Yang et al. (2022) consider an instance-wise selector that employs heterogeneous samples. Lee et al. (2022) takes a self-supervised way with unlabeled samples to enhance stochastic gates that encourage the model explainability meanwhile.” The terms introduced here (stochastic gates, heterogeneous samples) are not defined. The writing is unclear as well. 

* I found the description of the ‘Datasets and Benchmarks’ in 5.1 WHITE-BOX REGRESSION SIMULATION very unclear, making it hard to follow the experiments. 

* “our method significantly outperforms all other benchmarks.” I do not see any tests of significance. 

* Figure 5 I found unclear.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper proposes FeDualEx a federated primal-dual algorithm for solving distributed composite saddle point problems. The authors consider a homogeneous setting and provide convergence guarantees achieved by FeDualEx when the objective function is convex-concave. The authors also evaluate the proposed algorithm experimentally on synthetic and real datasets.
reviews.strengths: Overall. the paper is well written with the ideas clearly explained. The proposed algorithm is well-motivated and backed by strong theoretical guarantees. The experiments show the effectiveness of the proposed approach.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The authors have missed an important reference [R1] which considers a nonconvex composite optimization and develops Douglas-Rachford Splitting Algorithms for solving the problem. 

- The authors should also discuss [R2] and [R3] which consider a non-convex composite problem but in a decentralized setting and without local updates. Also, in contrast to the duality-based approach taken by the authors, the works [R2] and [R3] propose primal algorithms that directly update the parameters using proximal stochastic gradient descent. The dual approach proposed by the authors is justified by the "curse of primal averaging". A question I have is why the algorithms [R2] and [R3] seem to work even though they are primal algorithms.

- Why are the guarantees presented in the paper independent of the number of clients? The effect of the number of clients should be discussed after the main results. Importantly, does the proposed algorithm achieve linear speed-up with the number of clients in the network?

- In the initial part of the paper the authors refer to the distance-generating function to be strictly convex but later it is assumed to be strongly convex. It is advisable to call it strongly convex from the beginning. 

- Define $h_1$, $h_2$ in Definition 3. 

- After Definition 3, the authors mention that the previous approaches that add the composite term to the Bregman
divergence may not work for dual extrapolation as certain parts of the analysis break down. Can the authors be more specific about what they mean here?

[R1] Dinh et al., FedDR – Randomized Douglas-Rachford Splitting Algorithms for Nonconvex Federated Composite Optimization, 2021(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2103.03452.pdf)

[R2] Yan et al., Compressed Decentralized Proximal Stochastic Gradient Method for Nonconvex Composite Problems with Heterogeneous Data, 2023 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.14252.pdf)

[R3] Xiao et al., A One-Sample Decentralized Proximal Algorithm for Non-Convex Stochastic Composite Optimization, 2023 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.09766.pdf)
reviews.questions: See the weaknesses section above.

Summary:
The paper proposes FeDualEx a federated primal-dual algorithm for solving distributed composite saddle point problems. The authors consider a homogeneous setting and provide convergence guarantees achieved by FeDualEx when the objective function is convex-concave. The authors also evaluate the proposed algorithm experimentally on synthetic and real datasets.

Strengths:
Overall. the paper is well written with the ideas clearly explained. The proposed algorithm is well-motivated and backed by strong theoretical guarantees. The experiments show the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

Weaknesses:
- The authors have missed an important reference [R1] which considers a nonconvex composite optimization and develops Douglas-Rachford Splitting Algorithms for solving the problem. 

- The authors should also discuss [R2] and [R3] which consider a non-convex composite problem but in a decentralized setting and without local updates. Also, in contrast to the duality-based approach taken by the authors, the works [R2] and [R3] propose primal algorithms that directly update the parameters using proximal stochastic gradient descent. The dual approach proposed by the authors is justified by the "curse of primal averaging". A question I have is why the algorithms [R2] and [R3] seem to work even though they are primal algorithms.

- Why are the guarantees presented in the paper independent of the number of clients? The effect of the number of clients should be discussed after the main results. Importantly, does the proposed algorithm achieve linear speed-up with the number of clients in the network?

- In the initial part of the paper the authors refer to the distance-generating function to be strictly convex but later it is assumed to be strongly convex. It is advisable to call it strongly convex from the beginning. 

- Define $h_1$, $h_2$ in Definition 3. 

- After Definition 3, the authors mention that the previous approaches that add the composite term to the Bregman
divergence may not work for dual extrapolation as certain parts of the analysis break down. Can the authors be more specific about what they mean here?

[R1] Dinh et al., FedDR – Randomized Douglas-Rachford Splitting Algorithms for Nonconvex Federated Composite Optimization, 2021(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2103.03452.pdf)

[R2] Yan et al., Compressed Decentralized Proximal Stochastic Gradient Method for Nonconvex Composite Problems with Heterogeneous Data, 2023 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.14252.pdf)

[R3] Xiao et al., A One-Sample Decentralized Proximal Algorithm for Non-Convex Stochastic Composite Optimization, 2023 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.09766.pdf)

Questions:
See the weaknesses section above.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents FeDualEx, a federated primal-dual algorithm designed to solve distributed composite saddle point problems. The authors provide convergence guarantees in a homogeneous setting and validate their approach through experiments on synthetic and real datasets.

**Overall Assessment**

The paper is well-written, with clear explanations of the proposed algorithm and its theoretical underpinnings. The authors have made a compelling case for the need for FeDualEx, and the experimental results demonstrate its effectiveness. However, there are several areas that require attention to strengthen the paper.

**Strengths**

The paper's clarity and the algorithm's strong theoretical guarantees are notable strengths. The experimental evaluation is also a significant plus, as it showcases the algorithm's performance on various datasets.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions**

1. **Missing References and Related Work**: The authors have overlooked important references, such as [R1], which discusses nonconvex composite optimization and Douglas-Rachford Splitting Algorithms. Additionally, [R2] and [R3] explore non-convex composite problems in decentralized settings without local updates. Incorporating these references and addressing their relevance to FeDualEx would enhance the paper's context and demonstrate a more comprehensive understanding of the field.

2. **Convergence Guarantees and Client Impact**: The convergence guarantees presented are independent of the number of clients, which seems counterintuitive. The authors should discuss the implications of this and explore how the algorithm's performance is affected by the number of clients. This could involve additional experiments or theoretical analysis to clarify the relationship between client count and algorithm efficacy.

3. **Notation and Definitions**: Some notation, such as $h_1$ and $h_2$ in Definition 3, lacks clear definition. Ensuring that all notation is clearly explained and consistently used throughout the paper is essential for readability and understanding.

4. **Distance-Generating Function**: The distance-generating function is initially described as strictly convex but later assumed to be strongly convex. The authors should maintain consistent terminology and ensure that assumptions are clearly stated and justified.

5. **Justification of Approach**: The authors should provide a clearer justification for their dual approach, particularly in relation to previous methods for adding composite terms to the Bregman divergence. Addressing potential limitations and explaining why these approaches may not work for dual extrapolation would strengthen the paper.

6. **"Curse of Primal Averaging"**: The authors should discuss the 'curse of primal averaging' and provide a rationale for their dual approach. This will help situate FeDualEx within the broader context of federated optimization and highlight its advantages.

**Conclusion and Recommendation**

In conclusion, while the paper presents a well-motivated algorithm with strong theoretical guarantees and promising experimental results, it requires revisions to address the noted weaknesses. Specifically, the authors need to engage with related work more thoroughly, clarify the impact of the number of clients on the algorithm's performance, refine notation and definitions, and justify their approach more clearly.

Given these revisions are addressed, I believe the paper would make a valuable contribution to the field. Therefore, I support a rating of 6, marginally above the acceptance threshold, with the expectation that the authors will make significant revisions to strengthen their manuscript. 

**Rating: 6**

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors propose an Algorithm FeDualEx for solving composite saddle point problems under distributed settings. The proposed algorithm is inspired from the dual extrapolation algorithm while using a proximal operator which they define using the generalized Bregman divergence defined for saddle functions. They analyze this algorithm under homogeneous settings and derive its convergence rate for the duality gap. They also study the special cases when the number of clients equals 1, where the convergence rate of FeDualEx matches the existing rates known in the literature. The study also demonstrates that solving using the dual extrapolation has advantages of learning better sparse solutions than solving the primal.
reviews.strengths:
The paper studies federated learning of composite saddle point problems, for which there does not seem to be much existing work. The proposed convergence rates. 

(Novelty) The paper proposes a new bregman divergence for saddle functions and its associated proximal operator, which are used in the dual extrapolation steps. 

(Clarity) The main results are presented well and contrasted to the related ones. The experimental results illustrate the benefit of solving using the federated dual extrapolation over methods such as Federated Mirror Prox. The comparison to the sequential algorithms also help to position the contributions in relation to the existing work.
reviews.weaknesses:
The algorithm is similar that of Federated Dual Averaging (Yuan et.al.) while incorporating the dual extrapolation strategy over the newly defined Bregmen divergence and the proximal operators. The challenges associated with adapting the above strategy over FeDualAvg doesn't seem to be conveyed well in the paper. 

From the motivations perspective, some examples of practical setups which required distributed learning of saddle point formulations would be useful in appreciating the contributions better.

Some discussion on the relation between the variables z=(x,y) and ς would help since the former already includes a primal and dual pair. 

To improve the clarity, one may include convexity assumptions of the functions involved while the main problem is defined in (1).
